Arguments.prompt: Download the US state boundary shapefile from the US Census Bureau at https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_state_20m.zip. Unzip the archive and load the shapefile using geopandas. Filter the GeoDataFrame to the single row where the NAME field equals 'Oregon'. Reproject the Oregon geometry to EPSG:4326 (WGS84 geographic coordinates) if it is not already in that CRS. Compute the bounding box of the Oregon polygon as (min_lon, min_lat, max_lon, max_lat) using the total_bounds attribute. Save the Oregon state boundary as a GeoJSON file named OregonBoundary.geojson in EPSG:4326. Also download the US county boundary shapefile from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_county_5m.zip. Unzip and load it with geopandas. Filter to rows where the STATEFP field matches Oregon's STATEFP (which is '41'). Reproject to EPSG:4326. Save the Oregon county boundaries as OregonCounties.geojson in EPSG:4326. Upload both OregonBoundary.geojson and OregonCounties.geojson to the S3 data store under the folder OregonNDVI.

Download OregonBoundary.geojson from the S3 data store folder OregonNDVI. Load it with geopandas and extract the bounding box as (min_lon, min_lat, max_lon, max_lat) using total_bounds. This bounding box represents the Oregon state extent in EPSG:4326. Determine the past 12 calendar months relative to today's date: for each month from 12 months ago up to and including last month (do not include the current incomplete month), define the month's date range as the first day of that month to the last day of that month. For each of the 12 months, query the Microsoft Planetary Computer STAC API at https://planetarycomputer.microsoft.com/api/stac/v1 for the collection 'modis-13A1-061'. Use the pystac_client library to connect to the catalog at https://planetarycomputer.microsoft.com/api/stac/v1 with a StacApiIO modifier that signs items using the planetary_computer library's sign function. For each month, call catalog.search with parameters: collections=['modis-13A1-061'], bbox=[min_lon, min_lat, max_lon, max_lat], datetime='<YYYY-MM-DD>/<YYYY-MM-DD>' using the first and last day of that month, and limit=5. Collect all items returned. If no items are returned for a given month, skip that month gracefully and record it as unavailable. If items are returned, select the first item. For the selected item, sign it using planetary_computer.sign(item). Extract the href for the asset key '500m_16_days_NDVI' and the href for the asset key '500m_16_days_VI_Quality'. Save a JSON file named NDVIAssets.json containing a list of objects, one per available month, each with fields: 'month' (string in format 'YYYY-MM'), 'ndvi_href' (string URL), 'vi_quality_href' (string URL), and 'item_datetime' (string ISO datetime of the item). Upload NDVIAssets.json to the S3 data store under the folder OregonNDVI.

Download OregonBoundary.geojson and NDVIAssets.json from the S3 data store folder OregonNDVI. Load OregonBoundary.geojson with geopandas in EPSG:4326. Load NDVIAssets.json as a list of month records, each containing fields 'month' (YYYY-MM string), 'ndvi_href' (URL string), 'vi_quality_href' (URL string), and 'item_datetime' (ISO datetime string). For each month record in the list, perform the following steps: (1) Open the NDVI raster from ndvi_href using rasterio. The NDVI asset in the modis-13A1-061 collection stores raw integer values that must be scaled by multiplying by 0.0001 to convert to the standard NDVI range of approximately [-1, 1]. Read the first band as a float32 array. Apply the scale factor 0.0001 to all valid pixels. (2) Open the VI_Quality raster from vi_quality_href using rasterio. Read the first band as a uint16 array. The VI_Quality band uses a bitmask where bits 0-1 (the two least significant bits) encode overall pixel quality: 00 (value 0) = good quality, 01 (value 1) = marginal quality, 10 (value 2) = snow/ice, 11 (value 3) = cloudy. Extract bits 0-1 by computing quality_flag = vi_quality_array & 0b11. Mask out pixels where quality_flag is not equal to 0 (i.e., keep only pixels where quality_flag == 0, meaning good quality). Set masked-out pixels to NaN in the scaled NDVI array. (3) Clip the NDVI raster to the Oregon state boundary polygon from OregonBoundary.geojson using rasterio.mask.mask with crop=True and nodata=NaN. Reproject the Oregon boundary to match the raster CRS before masking if necessary using geopandas to_crs. After clipping, set any nodata values to NaN. (4) Compute the mean NDVI across all non-NaN pixels in the clipped, masked array using numpy nanmean. Record this scalar value as the mean statewide NDVI for this month. (5) Save the clipped, scaled, quality-masked NDVI array along with its rasterio transform and CRS as a GeoTIFF file named NDVI_<YYYY-MM>.tif (e.g., NDVI_2024-01.tif) using rasterio. Upload each NDVI_<YYYY-MM>.tif to the S3 data store under the folder OregonNDVI. After processing all months, create a JSON file named NDVIMeanStats.json containing a list of objects, one per processed month, each with fields: 'month' (YYYY-MM string), 'mean_ndvi' (float, the mean statewide NDVI), and 'item_datetime' (ISO datetime string). Upload NDVIMeanStats.json to the S3 data store under the folder OregonNDVI.

Download NDVIAssets.json and NDVIMeanStats.json from the S3 data store folder OregonNDVI. Load NDVIAssets.json to get the list of available months (each with a 'month' field in YYYY-MM format). For each available month, download the corresponding GeoTIFF file named NDVI_<YYYY-MM>.tif (e.g., NDVI_2024-01.tif) from the S3 data store folder OregonNDVI. Download OregonBoundary.geojson and OregonCounties.geojson from the S3 data store folder OregonNDVI. Load both with geopandas in EPSG:4326. Determine the number of available months N. Arrange subplots in a grid with enough rows and columns to fit N panels (use math.ceil(N/3) rows and min(N,3) columns, or a similar compact layout). Create a matplotlib figure of appropriate size (approximately 6 inches wide per column and 5 inches tall per row). For each month panel in order: (1) Open the NDVI_<YYYY-MM>.tif file using rasterio. Read the first band as a float32 array. Get the rasterio transform and CRS. (2) Reproject the raster to EPSG:4326 using rasterio.warp.reproject if it is not already in EPSG:4326, so that it aligns with the boundary overlays. (3) Use rasterio.plot.show or matplotlib imshow to display the NDVI raster on the subplot axes. Set vmin=-1 and vmax=1. Use the colormap 'YlGn'. (4) Overlay the Oregon state boundary from OregonBoundary.geojson as a black line with linewidth=1.5 using geopandas plot with ax parameter, facecolor='none', edgecolor='black'. (5) Overlay the Oregon county boundaries from OregonCounties.geojson as gray lines with linewidth=0.5 using geopandas plot with ax parameter, facecolor='none', edgecolor='gray'. (6) Set the subplot title to the month string formatted as '<Month Name> <Year>' (e.g., 'January 2024'). (7) Set axis limits to the Oregon bounding box extent from OregonBoundary.geojson total_bounds. (8) Label x-axis as 'Longitude' and y-axis as 'Latitude'. Add a single shared colorbar on the right side of the figure labeled 'NDVI' with range -1 to 1. Set the overall figure title to 'Monthly NDVI across Oregon (MODIS 500m, modis-13A1-061)'. Hide any unused subplot panels if N is not a perfect multiple of the grid dimensions. Save the figure as OregonNDVI_MapGrid.png at 150 DPI using matplotlib savefig with bbox_inches='tight'. Upload OregonNDVI_MapGrid.png to the S3 data store under the folder OregonNDVI.

Download NDVIMeanStats.json from the S3 data store folder OregonNDVI. Load it as a list of objects, each with fields 'month' (YYYY-MM string), 'mean_ndvi' (float), and 'item_datetime' (ISO datetime string). Sort the list by 'month' in ascending chronological order. Parse each 'month' string into a Python datetime object representing the first day of that month (e.g., datetime.strptime(month, '%Y-%m')). Extract the list of datetime objects as x-values and the list of mean_ndvi floats as y-values. Create a matplotlib figure of size (12, 5). Plot a line connecting all (datetime, mean_ndvi) points using matplotlib.pyplot.plot with color='green', linewidth=2, marker='o', markersize=6, markerfacecolor='darkgreen', markeredgecolor='black', markeredgewidth=0.8. Identify the month with the maximum mean_ndvi value (seasonal peak) and the month with the minimum mean_ndvi value (seasonal trough). Highlight the peak point by plotting it as a red star marker (marker='*', markersize=15, color='red', zorder=5) and annotating it with the text 'Peak: <YYYY-MM>\nNDVI=<value:.3f>' using matplotlib annotate with an arrow pointing to the point, textcoords='offset points', xytext=(10, 10), fontsize=9, color='red'. Highlight the trough point by plotting it as a blue star marker (marker='*', markersize=15, color='blue', zorder=5) and annotating it with the text 'Trough: <YYYY-MM>\nNDVI=<value:.3f>' using matplotlib annotate with an arrow pointing to the point, textcoords='offset points', xytext=(10, -20), fontsize=9, color='blue'. Add a horizontal dashed gray line at y=0 with linewidth=0.8 and linestyle='--' to indicate the zero NDVI baseline. Set the x-axis to display month labels using matplotlib.dates.MonthLocator and matplotlib.dates.DateFormatter('%b %Y'), rotating tick labels 45 degrees. Set the y-axis label to 'Mean NDVI'. Set the x-axis label to 'Month'. Set the y-axis limits to [-1, 1]. Add a grid with alpha=0.3 and linestyle='--'. Set the figure title to 'Mean Statewide NDVI over Oregon — Past 12 Months (MODIS modis-13A1-061)'. Add a legend with entries for 'Mean NDVI', 'Seasonal Peak', and 'Seasonal Trough'. Use tight_layout(). Save the figure as OregonNDVI_Timeseries.png at 150 DPI using matplotlib savefig with bbox_inches='tight'. Upload OregonNDVI_Timeseries.png to the S3 data store under the folder OregonNDVI.
